does: This paper systematically evaluates over 70 large language models varying across architectures, fine-tuning methods, and training datasets on a triplet similarity task using concepts from the THINGS database. The triplet task, a well-established cognitive science method, measures whether models develop human-like conceptual representations by asking them to judge which of two items is more similar to a reference item. The authors compare model performance against human similarity judgments to quantify representational alignment and identify which computational ingredients—architectural choices, training procedures, datasets—are most critical for achieving human-like concept representations in LLMs.
connects: This work bridges cognitive science and deep learning by adopting the triplet similarity paradigm from cognitive psychology to evaluate neural network representations. It builds on prior work establishing that transformer representations can capture meaningful semantic structure and extends existing representation alignment literature that has examined model-human agreement on various cognitive tasks. The paper responds to limitations of standard language model benchmarks, which typically measure downstream task performance rather than representational quality or human cognitive compatibility. It draws on the broader program of using cognitive science methods to understand and evaluate whether modern LLMs function as valid cognitive models, connecting to research on representation learning and the interpretability of neural networks.
enables: This work establishes a rigorous benchmark and evaluation framework for assessing representational alignment in LLMs, enabling future research to measure progress toward cognitively plausible models with greater precision than current task-based benchmarks. By identifying which computational ingredients matter most for human-like representations, the findings could guide more targeted design choices in model development. The approach opens directions for investigating how different training procedures, datasets, and architectural modifications affect the conceptual structure learned by models, potentially informing the design of LLMs that serve as better cognitive models. This framework could also support developing models with more aligned and interpretable representations, valuable for both cognitive science applications and safety considerations around model behavior.
